text: The surgeon begins the peritoneal flap dissection at the medial umbilical ligament using an energy device, carefully developing the peritoneal dissection while maintaining a pre-peritoneal approach to avoid entering the pre-transverse salus plane.
The surgeon maintains the visceral compartment by keeping the intermediate fascia against the rectus muscle to minimize bleeding.
The video shows the visceral compartment highlighted in green, providing a clear visual distinction from surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon considers transitioning to the parietal compartment, which would require moving anteriorly to the visible intermediate fascia above the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the intermediate fascia, progressing towards the lateral aspect of the surgical field.
The surgeon is currently positioned in the parietal compartment and is preparing to transition into the visceral compartment.
The surgeon exposes and navigates through the areolar tissue planes, revealing the distinct layers within the surgical field.
The surgeon highlights the boundaries of the visceral compartment, distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon incises the intermediate fascia to access the visceral compartment laterally, reflecting the fascia and areolar tissue towards the sidewall to protect the nerves and vasculature.
The surgeon is operating within the visceral compartment, with visible areolar tissue planes surrounding the area.
The surgeon carefully holds the peritoneum while reflecting the fatty tissue and areolar tissue up against the sidewall, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon prepares to address any indirect hernia sacs following a limited dissection performed medially and laterally.
The surgeon works on developing the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, focusing on the anatomical structures surrounding this area.
The surgeon incises the intermediate fascial layer, revealing underlying structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon positions over the bladder and focuses on accessing the parietal compartment, manipulating surrounding tissues to achieve entry.
The surgeon has entered the parietal compartment, with visible anatomical structures and tissue layers indicative of this surgical area.
The surgeon uses green highlights to visually delineate the parietal compartment, emphasizing its boundaries within the surgical field.
The surgeon reflects the intermediate fascia downwards towards the bladder, ensuring the bladder is protected during this portion of the dissection.

The surgeon continues retro pubic dissection to establish the critical view, focusing on identifying potential hernia sites in women, including femoral defects. A small preperitoneal fat femoral hernia is observed.
The surgeon reduces the hernia and proceeds to dissect the area to expose the lacunar ligament, revealing the anatomical structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully establishes the critical view during the dissection, ensuring clear visualization of anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, reflecting the surrounding tissue towards the sidewall to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the round ligament in preparation for transection, following the previous dissection of surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses energy to transect the round ligament, taking care to do so close to the peritoneum to minimize the risk of bleeding and avoid injury to the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve.
The surgeon tidies up the dissection area, ensuring that the mesh will have a smooth and secure landing zone against the anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the iliopubic tract and rolls out a cord lipoma, completing a light dissection to prepare the area for mesh positioning.
The surgeon places a mid-weight polypropylene mesh in the surgical field, achieving extensive coverage over the dissected area.
The surgeon places a single stitch through the lacunar ligament, adjacent to Cooper's ligament, preparing the area for secure mesh fixation.
The surgeon secures the mesh with two points of fixation, ensuring stable placement over the dissected area.
The surgeon performs a standard flap closure, reducing insufflation pressure to between six and eight millimeters of mercury, and proceeds to close the flap from lateral to medial.
